ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the working principle and loss mechanisms in a luminescent solar concentrator (LSC). The light-harvesting efficiency of the device strictly depends on the loss mechanism irrespective of the device configuration. (1) Luminophores, (2) incident light (diffused light) – normal and inclined incidence, (3) loss due to reflected light from the surface of LSC, (4) loss due to transmission of light, (5) primary emission from luminophores, (6) reabsorption leading to non-radiative decay, (7) secondary emission after reabsorption by the luminophores, (8) escape cone losses, (9) waveguided light reached to PV cells.

(a) The visual appearance of the Polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA, 350 k) thin film waveguides loaded with AuNPs at different concentrations on polycarbonate sheets and a layer of 20 µm PMMA/SiO2 on the top of it. (Reprinted with permission from Ref. (El-Bashir et al., 2014). (Copyright (2013) Elsevier Ltd.) (b) Image of a PLSC device of size 45 × 45 × 3 mm3 which is made with silicone encapsulant polymer by incorporating 70 ppm Red305 dye molecules and 1.1 ppm Au@Ag NCs. (Reprinted with permission from Ref. (Sethi et al., 2019). Copyright (2019) Elsevier B. V.).

Average PLQY as a function of wavelength and distance between surface of a metal sphere (radius of 50 nm) and a dye molecule which has intrinsic PLQY of 0.8. The simulation was done by FDTD method considering the radiative and non-radiative enhancement factors. The image is

(a) Schematic representation of LSC device with QDs as luminophore materials and the device is coated with 3D macroporous-structured PC on both front and back surface. The right-hand side schematic shows the cross-sectional view of LSC device and the photon trapping mechanisms. (b-c) the photon emissions from the edges (indicated by red arrows) are estimated from Monte-Carlo ray tracing method with and without the use of PC. (Reprinted with permission from Ref. (Wang et al., 2020). Copyright (2019) Elsevier Ltd.). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Prism coupling configuration of SPR, where p-polarized light absorbed by the surface plasmon as shown in the reflection spectra. (B) Representation of the LSPR on MNPs and absorbance spectra obtained for typical MNPs. Reprinted with permission from Ref. (Masson, 2020). Copyright (2020) Royal Society of Chemistry.

Schematics of PLSC design. MNPs are used in various way e.g. inside the waveguiding material along with luminophores and/or surface of the waveguiding materials. Thus, PLSC thickness will be thin film or of the order of mm thickness. All the devices described here are in the form of solid state. (a) waveguide loaded with only MNPs (Huang et al., 2019), (b) waveguide loaded with both luminophores and MNPs in the form of thin film (El-Bashir et al., 2013; Tummeltshammer et al., 2013) or standalone sheet (Sethi et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2014), (c) waveguide is loaded with luminophores and layer of MNPs are formed on the front surface and/or back surface of LSC. The waveguiding material may be in the form of thin film on an another substrate (glass or polymer) (El-Bashir et al., 2014) or a standalone waveguiding slab of thickness of the order of mm (Segura et al., 2020) loaded with luminophores. (d) stack structured PLSC device with a gap between the layers of thick waveguide (Mateen et al., 2017) and MNPs or thin film waveguide.

(a) Comparison of edge emission power of stacked structured LSC (SLSC) and stacked structured PLSC (SPLSC). Stacked structured LSC devices are like the configuration as shown in the Fig. 6(d). SPLSC-1: MNPs_1- 20 nm Ag NPs, MNPs_2- 20 nm Au NPs and SPLSC-2: MNPs_1- 60 nm Ag NPs, MNPs_2- 20 nm Au NPs (all MNPs are coated with PVP and concentration is maintained at 0.01 wt%). PMMA mixed with MACROLEX Fluorescent Yellow 10GN (0.8 wt%) and MACROLEX Fluorescent Red G (1 wt%) are used as waveguiding medium for upper and lower stack, respectively. (Adapted with permission from (Mateen et al., 2017) Copyright (2017) Elsevier Ltd.) (b)

ce:section.ce:para:
Solar energy harvesting strategies are recently becoming a thrust area of research to realize a goal of generating low-cost solar energy. The easiest approach is the use of less amount of semiconductor photovoltaic materials. This idea was first demonstrated in late 1970s (Goetzberger and Greube, 1977; Goetzberger and Wittwer, 1979; Weber and Lambe, 1976). The generic name, which was given to this device, is the luminescent solar concentrator (LSC). The area of the device for solar radiation collection is very large compared to the area of the semiconductor photovoltaic (PV) used at the edge i.e. high geometrical gain (G) can be achieved. The value of G of the order of ≈ 10 is appropriate for achieving a balance between efficiency and flux gain. Weber and Lambe (Weber and Lambe, 1976) first proposed that wave guiding of luminescence can enhance the photovoltaic solar energy conversion. The luminescent medium absorbs the incoming radiations and total internal reflection helps the emissions to travel through the waveguide to collect at the edge of LSC device. Since then several theoretical modelling and experimental demonstrations (Assadi et al., 2016; Vasiliev et al., 2019) are reported to accomplish the improvement of overall performance, lowering the cost, increasing external quantum efficiency (
η
ext
, the ratio of photon flux coming from the edge and the total incident solar flux) of the LSC which is linked to the concentration factor (C) or effective concentration factor (ECF) by C
or
ECF =
η ext
× G . Monte Carlo modeling as well as experimental demonstrations of QDs based LSCs shows that the value of C (always<100) is connected to the LSC quality factor i.e. ratio of absorption coefficients at incident wavelength and emission wavelength (Klimov et al., 2016).

In the past fifty years, scientists have demonstrated the LSC device using different types of organic dye molecules (Rafiee et al., 2019), upconverted materials (Ha et al., 2018; Nam et al., 2020), rare earth complexes (Day et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2011), perovskite nanocrystals (Tong et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020), fluorescent proteins (Sadeghi et al., 2019) as the luminescent species (Mazzaro and Vomiero, 2018; Meinardi et al., 2017a) including green alternatives (Z. Li et al., 2019). Actual number of reports are huge compared to the references cited here. The main advantage of organic dyes is high photoluminescence quantum efficiency (PLQY) and its absorption from ultraviolet (UV) to near infrared (NIR). Whereas the upconverted materials and rare earth ions have low absorption window and cannot be tuned easily to absorb maximum amount of solar spectrum. Different types of QDs, which emits from UV to NIR, have demonstrated in LSC devices after extensive research on the improvement of emission QY during last twenty years. QDs have benefits over dyes molecules in several ways – broad absorption spectrum, absorption and emission properties can be adjusted by the size of the QDs, superior in stability, and the tunable Stoke shift to circumvent re-absorption losses. During last five years, researchers are trying to improve the efficiency of LSC with QDs, engineered QDs, altering the PV attached and other strategies so that the device can be used commercially (Mateen et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018). In the recent past, the plasmonic came into the picture, and researchers are trying to improve the efficiency of the QDs-LSC device with the help of enhanced fluorescence by metal nanostructure. Still, the performance of plasmonic LSC (PLSC) devices is in its infancy and extensive research is required to improve the performance as well as making it cost effective too.

In a recent review, Yang et. al. discussed the theoretical limits of transparent LSC device (Yang and Lunt, 2017). They also identified the key parameters for the improvement of the device and paved a new pathway to build efficient transparent LSC. However, there are many practical challenges to make ideal emitter with high QY, optimal bandgap, narrow emission, and large Stoke shift. Meinardi et al (Meinardi et al., 2017a) reviewed the LSC for building-integrated photovoltaics which might be available in the market near future. They focused their discussion on colloidal QDs with small amount of self-absorption effect and QY of unity with broad spectral absorption. They mentioned in the review that highly performing optical waveguides is not optimized yet. Hence, the role of absorption and scattering of the waveguide will soon become an active research area to use LSCs for real-world applications. Merkx et al described the multiple-generation light transport (MGLT) which is based upon Beer-Lambert’s law to quantify light transport through single-layer LSC of maximum area of 1 m2 (neglecting the scattering through LSC) (Merkx et al., 2017). This model needs less computation power over the traditional method of Monte Carlo (raytracing). Both simulation methods show that the size of the LSC device plays an important role for the optimization of the concentration of luminophores. Therefore, lab demonstration of optimized LSC cannot be applicable in large scale production of LSC which would have different size and shape for commercial use. The maximum power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 1 m2 LSCs has a theoretical value of 9% and experimental value of 6% for luminophores (Lumogen Red 305 and QDs). Theoretically, it was demonstrated that sometime the overlap between absorption and emission spectrum of QDs with high QY can be advantageous to the final optical efficiency (Merkx et al., 2017).

The focus of this review article is mainly on the recent progress of PLSC device. The review starts with a discussion of the working principle of LSC device. Then, it advances to the brief discussion on common loss mechanisms of LSC device which restrict its efficiency to be poor. Here, we only discuss about the loss mechanisms which arise from the chromophore/waveguide materials, neglecting the shape and geometry of the device and solar cell materials. We describe the possible remedies to overcome the losses in LSC device. We discuss the topics like optimization of the chromophores, spectral coverage, color of the LSC panels, strategies to maximize the efficiency, optimization of the waveguide, etc. We discuss also in detail about the enhancement of efficiency of PLSC due to plasmonic nanoparticles. Finally, we summarize the status of research on the improvement of PLSC device and discuss the future strategies to optimize the efficiency of PLSC device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operating principles and potential loss process
ce:section.ce:para: The simplest form of LSC is defined as a waveguide doped with highly emissive luminophores (e.g. QDs, dye molecules) attached with a solar cell at the edge. The operation of LSC consists of three main processes - (i) incident light absorption on the surface, (ii) emission followed by absorption of incoming photons inside LSC and (iii) emitted photon (first emission or emission after successive re-absorption events) transport to the edges of the LSC as illustrated in the Fig. 1 . A conventional LSC is a nearly transparent rectangular waveguide of high refractive index (n ~ 1.5 or more) in which both direct and diffuse sunlight are absorbed and reemitted at Stoke shifted wavelength. Some photons are getting total internally reflected several times and travelled to the edges of LSC device. The transported photons are getting absorbed by the photovoltaic cells (PV) which are optically coupled as shown in Fig. 1. LSC can be divided into many groups according to their architecture and luminescent materials: (i) luminophore materials (e.g. QDs, dye molecule) are dispersed inside the waveguide matrix, (ii) one or multiple layer of luminophore materials coated on the top/bottom of LSC waveguide, (iii) one or multiple layer of luminophore materials sandwiched between two waveguide plates, (iv) multi-color LSC connected in stacked layer format.

The geometric gain factor or geometric gain or geometric factor (G) is the ratio of the surface area of the LSC exposed to sunlight/diffused light and the area of the PV attached to the edge of LSC device. The light concentration efficiency of LSCs mostly depends on three factors: the geometrical gain (G), the optical efficiency of the concentrator (
η
opt
), and the efficiency of the PV cell (
η
PV
). In the literature, there are several expression and name for efficiency of same origin. Here, we discuss the parameters/efficiencies/factors and their aliases in the simplest manner which will characterize the LSC efficiently. For LSC device, we define the internal quantum efficiency (
η
int
) or the collection efficiency (
η
col
) or quantum optical efficiency as the ratio of number of photons emitted from the edges of the LSC (may or may not absorbed by the PV) to the number of photons absorbed by the LSC. On the other hand, the external quantum efficiency (
η

η

or OE) as the ratio of number of photons collected by the PV from the edges of the LSC to the number of photons incident (may or may not absorbed by the LSC) on the surface of LSC. If the absorption efficiency of the LSC device is

Rafiee et. al. has been comprehensively reviewed the PCE values which varies for large and small scale LSCs as well as with the variation of the configuration of the LSC devices (Rafiee et al., 2019).

The PCE of LSCs decreases with the length of LSC device (~few cm) because it leads to multiple loss mechanisms as shown in Fig. 1. The main loss mechanisms are re-absorption losses, escape cone losses, limited spectral efficiency and losses arises from the spectral mismatch between the LSC and the PV. However, the

where

is the reabsorption probability of emitted photon by the luminophores (Batchelder et al., 1981). For every reabsorption event, extra loss pathways can be introduced as in the case of primary absorption–emission event including escape-cone losses and non-unity PLQY losses (Huang et al., 2019).

In plasmonic LSC devices, metal nanoparticles or nanocrystals (MNP) are either randomly distributed (homogenous composite) or form a layer/stack of MNPs or thin reflecting layer of bulk metal on the back side (Mateen et al., 2017). A broadband plane wave source (the wavelength of incoming photons is extremely long comparing to the size of MNPs) as in solar spectrum produces strongly confined plasmon oscillations which produces strong electromagnetic (EM) fields around the MNPs (Pastoriza-Santos et al., 2018) and hence, interacts with the luminophores particles inside the LSC. When luminophores are in the vicinity of EM field, plasmonic interaction happens which can alter the absorption probability, the excitation rate, radiative and non-radiative decay rates (Anger et al., 2006; Carminati et al., 2006). Local field enhancement can be easily noticed around sharp parts of MNPs (corner for triangular MNPs, edge for nanorod, tips for star shaped MNPs) as a consequence of surface plasmon (SP) in metals (Yu et al., 2019). SP is the effect of an interaction between photon and electrons. Usually the plasmon frequency (
ω p
) is directly proportional to the square root of the density of carriers (electrons or holes) i.e.
ω p
∝
N
(West et al., 2010). The frequency of SP for noble metals, alloyed metals like Au and Ag falls in the visible region whereas it falls in the region of NIR, IR for degenerate semiconductors. Moreover, it would be possible to obtain maximum enhancement for both the excitation and emission of a luminophore material by utilizing both transverse and longitudinal plasmonic mode as demonstrated in Au and Ag MNPs (Chen et al., 2010, 2007; Liu et al., 2013; Yu et al., 2019). The general equation for PL intensity enhancement factor (FPL) due to presence of MNPs (Anger et al., 2006; Guzatov et al., 2012) is given by

is its modified quantum due to MNP. On the other hand, maximum enhancement of excitation and emission of a luminophore material will be determined by the shape and size of MNPs for the localized SPR (LSPR) effect (Zhuang et al., 2019). This effect requires very short distance between MNPs and luminophore of the order of ~ 10 nm for enhancement. However, “far-field” surface plasmon resonance (FSPR) has attracted much attention due to its high enhancing yield which mainly comes from the interaction between emission of luminophores and emission which is reflected by the substrates (Zhuang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Some strategies to maximize the efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
The LSC is a promising solar concentrator technology to realize low cost photovoltaics alternative, building-integrated PV (BIPV). Moreover, the color and transparency of LSC device can be altered by tuning the type, emission peak and concentration of luminophores, which can meet the specific requirements of BIPVs (Vasiliev et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2018). However, getting high
η
opt
is a challenging task as it suffers from several energy losses mechanisms such as escape cone, reabsorption, attenuation, and scattering losses (Tummeltshammer et al., 2016). According to the discussions in the previous section [Eq. (4) & Eq. (9)], the overall efficiency will be maximum when the following ideal conditions will be satisfied: (i)
η
PLQY
≅ 1
, (ii)
η
PV
is equal to the Shockley–Queisser limit, (iii)
R ≅ 0
, (iv)
η
TIR
≅ 1
, (v)
η
RA
= 1
. We will never be able to reach the ideal condition rather we would try to optimize the parameters to get maximum possible PCE for LSC.

High quality luminophores are important ingredients for producing LSC device. Luminophores must be stable at higher temperature, highly luminescent (
η
PLQY
≈ 1
), non-toxic and cost effective (Purcell-Milton and Gun’ko, Y.K., 2012) to make it commercially feasible and suitable for BIPV system. Moreover, luminophores must have high quantum yield and large Stokes-shift owing to the minimum reabsorption losses. The emission properties of luminophores must retain in its original condition (or with minimum amount of loss) inside the waveguiding matrices so that maximum PCE can be achieved for LSC device. The luminophores must have the ability to sustain at harsh environment as hot as ~ 50 °C (~122 °F). However, the overall efficiency of LSC device will be hindered due to the heat developed inside LSC functioning in the sunlight. Researchers have explored LSC and PLSC devices by incorporating several luminophores and MNPs including QDs, engineered QDs, organic dyes, metal oxides nanoparticles (NPs), metal complexes, semiconducting polymers, dye sensitized C60 and even protein complexes to maximize the efficiency of LSC.

Organic dye molecules such as Coumarin and Rhodamine are the first luminophores which was used in the LSC device in 70́s (Batchelder et al., 1980; Currie et al., 2008; Weber and Lambe, 1976) till the luminescent QDs discovered. Organic dye molecules are the most exploited luminophores in LSC devices due to its high quantum yield and good solubility in polymer matrices (Debije and Verbunt, 2012). In general, dye molecules can convert only a small part of the solar spectrum due to its narrow absorption band and small Stokes-shift. Furthermore, organic dyes suffer from photo-degradation effect and spectral mismatch with PV attached to LSC. These are the reasons behind the non-availability of commercial LSC device with dye molecules for the time being (Debije, 2017; Debije and Verbunt, 2012; Moraitis et al., 2018). Researchers are trying to alter the spectral coverage of dye molecules with the help of foreign elements which would lead to instability of the dye structure and eventually reduces PLQY. Lanthanide complexes are photodegraded due to UV band in solar spectrum as well as it possesses low thermal and photochemical stability which limits its use in any technological implementation (Debije and Verbunt, 2012).

But, very significant and interesting lumiphores is QDs (typically<10–20 nm) which demonstrate a range of unique properties depending on their size and shape (Bradshaw et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020; Moraitis et al., 2018; Purcell-Milton and Gun’ko, Y.K., 2012). QDs offer several advantages over other luminophores, including straightforward wet chemistry approaches for synthesis (Pu et al., 2018; Reiss et al., 2016, 2009; van Embden et al., 2015), high PLQY, broad absorption spectrum (300–2000 nm), size-controlled widely tunable emission and absorption spectra (Pietryga et al., 2016), enhanced environmental stability and photostability compared with other luminophores. Engineering the thickness of the shell, the LSC, which is made QDs with thick shell, exhibit high
η
opt
with reduced reabsorption loss (Coropceanu and Bawendi, 2014). To be more specific, LSC devices are mostly demonstrated with II–VI cadmium chalcogens which are widely investigated QDs over the past year. Still, there are some concerns about the use of QDs which are made up with toxic elements including Cd (CdS, CdSe, CdTe), Pb (PbS, PbSe, PbTe), Hg (HgS, HgSe, HgTe) and As (GaAs, InAs). The simplest approach to overcome this problem is to make a shell layer of non-toxic semiconductor compound like CdSe/ZnSe, CdS/ZnS and CdSe/CdS/ZnS which could reduce the toxicity of a QDs. Otherwise, QDs can be coated with a non-toxic polymer to reduce the overall toxicity e.g. gelatin coated CdTe (Byrne et al., 2007), etc. Another approach is to make green and eco-friendly QDs to use in commercial LSC devices (Liu et al., 2020; Reiss et al., 2016; You et al., 2019). In recent times, researchers are using green luminophores like Si (Meinardi et al., 2017b), C dots (Gong et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2019; Mateen et al., 2019b; Zhao, 2019), SiO2 (Liu et al., 2018), CuInS2 and CuInSe2 (Hu et al., 2015), CuInSe2/ZnS (Bergren et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2018), CuInSexS2−x/ZnS (Meinardi et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2018), CuInS2/ZnS (Bergren et al., 2018; Li et al., 2016; Neo et al., 2020), CuInSeS/ZnS (Meinardi et al., 2015), Ag/SiO2 (Segura et al., 2020), AgInS2/ZnS (Chen et al., 2017), Ag2S, Ag2Se, Ag2S/Se (Liu et al., 2020), InP/ZnSe (Sadeghi et al., 2020), thiolated gold-nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) (Huang et al., 2019), fluorescent Proteins (Sadeghi et al., 2019), natural dye molecules like chlorophyll (Frias et al., 2018) and many more. However, there are significant growth in QDs based LSC over the last five years. Still, the device suffers low PCE (<10%) due to low QYs of QDs in polymer waveguide matrixes, reabsorption loss, and lacks long-term stability. In addition, for the purpose of commercialization of QDs based LSC device, we can take the complete benefit of QDs as luminophores in combination with plasmonic micro/nanoparticles in PLSC device.

In general, the concentration of luminophores in LSCs is strongly restricted to < 1 wt% to minimize the concentration-induced quenching (CIQ), reabsorption losses, aggregation-induced scattering (AIS) (Huang et al., 2019; Meinardi et al., 2017a; Sumner et al., 2017) and thus controls the value of final PCE. Lowering the concentration eventually induces the transmission loss for thin film LSC device. The concentration of luminophores should increase (particularly for luminophores with narrow-absorption band) for thin film LSC device so that one can obtain considerable light harvesting efficiency. But the concentration has limit as it provokes low miscibility and phase segregation. The back-scattered layers or back-reflected mirrors can be applied to recover the transmitted light from the back surface for the thin film LSC which has luminophores at low concentration (Day et al., 2019; Debije et al., 2009; Schrecengost et al., 2018). Therefore, the above techniques allow us to develop thin-film LSCs with high concentration (≈26 wt%) by using luminophores with low absorption window (Huang et al., 2019).

Thermodynamic principles show that LSCs should absorb all the incoming photons but should not reabsorb the emitted photons by the luminophores to achieve maximum concentration of light (Papakonstantinou and Tummeltshammer, 2015) and hence the edge emission may be improved. At large concentration, the luminophores form an aggregates which produce new non-radiative decay paths and eventually lower the effective QY of the system (Zhao et al., 2019). Moreover, the reabsorption events are accelerated as the concentration of luminophore materials increase. The efficiency and concentration factor increase with the concentration of luminophores inside LSC, but optimal concentration should be considered for the efficacy of the device (Krumer et al., 2017; Sumner et al., 2017; Tummeltshammer et al., 2016). On the contrary, the performance of LSCs does not depend on the concentration in liquid phase LSC with luminescent dye molecules (Krumer et al., 2017). However, in the context of LSC as a BIPV elements, the transparency of the LSC (alters with concentration of luminophores) decides the color quality of sunlight in the interior of a building (Aste et al., 2017, 2015; Moraitis et al., 2019). The visual appearance of the PLSC devices is shown in the Fig. 2 which indicates that the transparency/color depends on the concentration of luminophores and MNPs. The waveguiding material itself might alter the transparency of the device.

For a PLSC device, the fluorescence enhancement or quenching exclusively depends on the concentration of the MNPs where the optimal concentration is determined by the coupling and spacing between neighboring QDs and MNPs (Chandra et al., 2012). Therefore, the concentration of the luminophores play an important role in the final value of PCE of a PLSC device. Moreover, surface plasmon is used to enhance the emission of the dye molecules in PLSC device allowing the use of potentially lower PLQY luminophores (Holland and Hall, 1985; Wilson, 1986). Apart from the addition of MNPs for plasmonic enhancement in LSC, the concentration of luminophores must be tuned to an optimized value to mitigate concentration-induced losses and obtain a reasonable PCE.

The host/waveguide materials have direct effect on the performance of LSC device. It is directly linked with loss mechanism of LSC device because the light-trapping efficiency (
η
TIR
), surface reflections (the value of R), absorption, and scattering losses (not yet fully understood) are closely linked to the nature of the material of the waveguide (Tatsi et al., 2020; Zettl et al., 2017). Therefore, wave guiding materials must have special properties, including suitable refractive index (optimum
n wg
≈
2.0 , while most common compromised value
n wg
≈
1.5 ), high transmittance in the visible and NIR region, high thermodynamic (or equilibrium) solubility, compatible with all weather conditions, durability, cost-effective, ease of optical coupling to PV cells (Griffini, 2019; Y. Li et al., 2019; Zettl et al., 2017). The wave guiding materials must act as a compatible host for common luminophores e.g. QDs, dye molecules, rare earth complex, perovskites. Theoretical studies have shown that the emission is anisotropic though the luminophores are isotropically distributed inside the wave guiding materials (Verbunt et al., 2013). In Section 2, it is noted that the best host materials must have optimized condition for the surface reflection and trapping efficiency, which requires
1 - R
η
TIR
to be maximum. But no polymer wave guiding material is ideal. Most common polymers are polyacrylates (Cambié et al., 2019; Gong et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Meinardi et al., 2014; Segura et al., 2020), polysiloxanes (Cambié et al., 2017; Gajic et al., 2017), polystyrene (PS) (Liu et al., 2019) and polycarbonates (PC) (El-Bashir and Al-Jaghwani, 2020; El-Bashir et al., 2014) which are used as wave guiding materials for LSCs.

Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) is favorable host polymer for organic dyes, rare-earth complexes with polar ligands due to its relatively polar nature. But it is not favorable for all type of QDs. It may produce aggregation of QDs which is generally coated with nonpolar hydrophobic organic ligands, such as trioctylphosphine/trioctylphosphine oxide (TOP/TOPO), octadecylamine (ODA), tributylphosphin (TBP), dodecanethiol (DDT) etc. which are used to maintain high quantum yield of QDs (Hühn et al., 2017; Reiss et al., 2016). That is why, poly(lauryl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) i.e. P(LMA-co-EDGMA) is a substitute of PMMA for the QD-based LSCs. P(LMA-co-EDGMA) can also be applied for thin film LSC. P(LMA-co-EDGMA) can form hard and soft device by changing the ratio of monomer and oligomer. On the other hand, polycarbonates are common polymer for thin-film LSCs owing to its high refractive index of 1.7 with ~ 81% trapping efficiency of incident light. Other polymers are also used in LSCs, for example, polyacrylamide (PAA) (Mulder et al., 2009), poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) (Zdražil et al., 2020), L-poly(lactic acid) (PLA) (Fattori et al., 2011), Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) (Wu et al., 2018), Epoxy resins (Y. Li et al., 2019). Addition to these polymers described above, some co-polymers, environmentally friendly, bio-based materials, organic–inorganic hybrid matrices, multifunctional matrices are also demonstrated in LSCs considering their excellent stability, durability, more favorable dye/matrix interactions, retaining properties of PLQYs of QDs inside matrix. More recently, P(LMA-co-EDGMA) (Coropceanu and Bawendi, 2014), chlorotrifluoro-ethylenevinyl-ether (CTFE–VE) with an aliphatic isocyanurate crosslinker (Griffini et al., 2013), poly(p-xylylene) (or parylene) (Maggioni et al., 2013), silk fibroin (SF) (Melucci et al., 2012), polysiloxane-rubber (Buffa et al., 2012), UV-curable fluoropolymeric matrix (Pintossi et al., 2017) and many more are used as wave guiding material for LSCs (Griffini, 2019).

Among these waveguide materials, a few [PMMA, PVP, P(LMA-co-EDGMA)] are being used in PLSC device so far because they are probably easily available and used in the previous literature. Commonly, researchers are using the same waveguiding material for both luminophores and MNPs in PLSC devices. Likewise, other waveguiding materials are also compatible with the MNPs which is useful for thin film PLSC device e.g. PVA (Levchenko et al., 2011). Several review articles encompass the key concepts related to metal nanoparticles embedded into the polymer matrix (Dzhardimalieva and Uflyand, 2018; Pastoriza-Santos et al., 2018; Prakash et al., 2015). The major limitation to make polymer composite of MNPs is that MNPs are usually carried out in solvents and show a spectral shifts for longitudinal and transverse surface plasmon resonance absorption in response to changes in their environment including the refractive index, temperature, redox potential, and chemical adsorption (Ghosh and Pal, 2007; Kelly et al., 2003; Mulvaney, 1996). Therefore, the absorption and emission properties of both luminophores and MNPs must be tuned in such a way that the emission enhancement will occur inside the waveguiding materials of PLSC device.

Device architecture is an important aspect of making LSC device. The overall performance will depend on the final form of functional structure of the device. LSC device may show different PCE with same luminophores but somewhat different from architectural aspect. Among all available designs, tandem configuration is the simplest approach to successfully enhance the PCE. In general, the tandem structure LSCs are made of two or three layers of waveguiding materials loaded with different luminophores according to their absorption properties. It can be made up with more than three layers in some case, but this will not be helpful for practical application due to its spectral mismatch with the solar cell affixed at the edge of LSC device. A comprehensive review of configurations and achieved performance in terms of PCE of LSC device are summarized in Ref. (Rafiee et al., 2019). It shows that the highest PCE is accomplished for PLSC which is made of dyes coupled with MNPs (El-Bashir et al., 2013, 2014). From the technical point of view, a multiple stack of LSCs layers, known as a tandem LSC, are used to reduce the escape cone losses by spectrally separating the solar spectrum and hence improve the efficiency of the device. Simultaneous use of carbon QDs and organic dyes in tandem LSC can show more than three times enhancement in the optical conversion efficiency and/or power conversion efficiency compared with single layer LSCs (Mateen et al., 2019a). Silver and gold NPs on the surface of wave guiding medium with different luminophores in a stacked structured LSC shows the maximum power efficiency of 1.7 (Mateen et al., 2017).

The ‘resonance shifting’ is a technique to overcome the propagation or attenuation loss in LSC largely due to multiple reabsorption. Authors show that the concentration ratio (CR, the ratio of radiant flux in the output to input) increases two-fold for different luminophores having less amount of reabsorption losses (Giebink et al., 2011). Resonance-shifting is the most effective technique for thin film LSC of thickness less than micron, and luminophores (e.g. organic chromophores or colloidal QDs) with absorption lengths of a few hundred nanometers (Currie et al., 2008). Monte Carlo ray tracing technique shows that CR value of ~ 16 can be achievable by utilizing meta-mirrors and Bragg mirrors even with significant reflectivity reducing escape cone losses in LSC. This strategy would be advantageous for lower quality or imperfect luminophore materials with QY less than ~ 70% (Connell and Ferry, 2016). A theoretical and experimental demonstration is reported which shows the use of distributed Bragg reflector (DBR) on the surface of LSC to recover the escaped luminescence with the help of spin-coated alternating layers of SiO2 and SnO2 NPs (Xu et al., 2016). Photonic bandgap filters, which have refection coefficient of 1 for the emission wavelengths and 0 for all other wavelengths, are also used to minimize the escape cone losses in LSC (Debije et al., 2010). When we talk about PLSC, the devices can be categorized into four groups according to the use of MNPs as shown in the Fig. 6 which is described later in the Section 4. Evanescent wave and localized field enhancement due to bulk metal/MNPs can be utilized to improve the absorption/emission properties of luminophores and overcome re-absorption losses in the PLSC device. More discussion will be in the next sections on PLSC device.

Simulation is an essential technique to estimate or optimize the PCE for LSC/PLSC prototype devices. Maxwelĺs equations clearly describes the absorption, scattering and transport of surface plasmon (SP) in metal nanostructures. Several computational packages or numerical approaches, which are based on Mie Theory, the transition matrix ‘null-field’ method (T-Matrix) discrete dipole approximation (DDA), finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) or Yee's method, finite element method (FEM), accurately estimate the optical interaction of SP with luminophores by calculating the absorption, extinction and scattering cross-section in presence of metal nanostructures. Mie theory, the oldest simulation technique, is only applicable to metal NPs with spherically symmetric geometries. Similarly, T-Matrix method is generally used to model the optical response of arrays of symmetric particles. DDA is applicable for both dielectric and metallic nanostructures. FEM modelling and the FDTD method are much faster than DDA method in terms of computation time and the required hardware. Among all, FDTD, a time domain technique, is used to study the light matter interaction in photonic-plasmonic system of arbitrary symmetry and geometry due to its handy and efficient approach with simple parameterization for estimating the optical response. FDTD can be used as a nanoscale modelling tool for PLSC device with plasmonic MNPs and luminophores (treated as point dipole source) as well as other system like plasmonic solar cells or liquid crystal displays, etc. FDTD may also be used to explore the reflectance, absorption loss in a waveguiding materials. To calculate the PCE for PLSC device, we need some macroscopic simulation tools like ray tracing model, which can manage the interaction and fate of huge number of incident photon for PLSC device. In recent years, several theoretical models, thermodynamic models, and the ray tracing models have been developed. All of them are trying to optimize the parameters of LSC device using different type of luminophores as well as waveguiding materials. The luminous output of an LSC loaded with dye molecules was theoretically modelled and accurately predicted light-to-light efficiency in terms of dye concentration and LSC length (Swift et al., 1999). Numerical modelling of LSC device has its own benefits and drawbacks (Aghaei et al., 2020; Parel et al., 2015; Song et al., 2018; Tummeltshammer et al., 2013; van Sark, 2013; Wiegman and van der Kolk, 2012).

Detailed balanced model (Shockley and Queisser, 1961) and Monte Carlo ray-tracing model (Reiher, 1966) are used mostly for LSC device simulation where the path of each incident photon is followed, and the photon counts measured at the edge of the device to calculate the efficiency (PCE/
η
TIR
). Monte Carlo ray tracing model is useful to optimize the PLQY, LSC efficiency, expected electrical output due to the probabilistic nature of reflection, transmission, absorption, emission, entrapment, self-absorption, and photo-conversion (Wilton, 2012). In the ray tracing model, the settings such as anti-reflection coating, back mirror/reflector configuration, several polymer waveguiding material, types of solar cell, variety of luminophores and the major loss factors can be readily introduced in order to verify their effects on PCE (Sark et al., 2008). A new model must be developed to consider the angular dependence of the incoming photon flux as well as the exiting flux at the LSC edge, all possible loss mechanism, device architecture. The combination of 3D Monte Carlo ray tracing (MCRT) algorithm along with 3D-FDTD method may be useful to optimize and investigate both LSC and PSLC device. A nanoscale modeling tool like FDTD method is used to visualize field enhancement in presence of MNPs as shown in the Fig. 3 .

MCRT in combination with FDTD methods were demonstrated in dye molecules based LSC device (
5 × 5 × 0.5
cm3) with single plasmonic layer of Ag NPs (sphere, shell, and disc configuration) and the outcome of the simulation confirms that the enhancement in PCE is mostly due to scattering from plasmonic particles (Tummeltshammer et al., 2013). MCRT method was verified in QDs based LSC device with macro-porous photonic crystal (PC) filter as reflector which is shown in the Fig. 4 . The simulation data predicts ~ 13-fold enhancement of
η
opt
(Wang et al., 2020) with PC reflector. A similar approach can be applied for PLSC device by considering the plasmonic enhancement of luminophores and loss mechanisms due to metal nanostructures for estimation of PCE of PLSC device. Thus, MCRT technique is quite useful to calculate the side emission of transported photons in PLSC device and hence one can determine the
η
int
or
η
col
.

Here, the fundamental idea of plasmonic LSC (PLSC) involves a technique which improves the PCE of common LSC device by improving the emission of luminophores interacting with metal/MNPs through processes known as Metal Enhanced Fluorescence (MEF) (Knoblauch and Geddes, 2019), Surface enhanced Fluorescence (SEF) (Fort and Grésillon, 2008) or Shell-isolated Nanoparticle-enhanced Fluorescence (SHINEF) (Fang et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018). When a luminophore is in proximity to the MNPs or the metal surface, the optical characteristics of luminophores may differ from its intrinsic properties (Sethi et al., 2019). First, the emission intensity of luminophore may increase due to local field enhancement near the luminophores formed in the vicinity of MNPs. Second, the radiative and non-radiative decay rate of luminophores may change and hence, the PLQY will increase/decrease accordingly. Third, Förster like quenching of emission intensity happens for luminophores near MNPs (<5 nm). To understand the interaction of luminophores and surface plasmons in MNPs i.e. the coupling of QDs to metal surfaces, we consider a point-like dipole source interacting with a flat metal surface or an oscillating dipole interacts with a conducting metal surface (Temnov and Woggon, 2012). Let us consider that the thickness of a dielectric spacer layer between the optical dipole and the metal surface is d in an experimental condition. The total normalized decay rate ( γ ) is the sum of all partial decay rates as described by the equation below (Ford and Weber, 1984; Temnov and Woggon, 2012).

γ
LSW

contribution is primarily due to electron-hole excitations and it dominates for low values of d. It is also evident that PL quenching is observed for luminophores near (d < 10 nm) metal surfaces from multiple experimental observation. The normalized decay rate via surface plasmon emission is the most important at intermediate distances of 10–100 nm limited by surface plasmon propagation length. It is worth mentioning that the decay time ( τ ) always measures the inverse decay rate ( γ ).

The plasmon or surface plasmon (SP) is a quanta of surface charge density oscillations of free electrons near a metal surface. It is provoked by incident light in accordance with special optical conditions (e.g. for incidence of p-polarized light) not likely from silver surfaces or mirrors illuminated by light. The combination of surface plasmon and coupled photon is called a surface plasmon polariton (SPP) comes from the optically induced electron oscillations and it propagates along the interface between metal and dielectric. SPP can couple luminophores at a long distance up to several micrometer by changing the wavelength of the incident light. The electro-magnetic (EM) field in the direction perpendicular to the interface is transverse magnetic in character. The decay length of the EM evanescent field is about half of wavelength of the incident light inside the dielectric medium and equal to the skin depth inside the metal. Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) occurs when the dimension of the metallic film is much greater than the wavelength of the incident light and involves a prism or waveguide to excite the plasmon in the interface. The unusual field enhancement and confinement produced by SP can extensively alter emission characteristics of point dipole source such as QDs, dye molecules, up-conversion centers. This is also called SP coupled emission (SPCE) which is different from MEF (the rate of radiative/non-radiative transition alteration involved). The electronic transition probability of such dipole source is directly proportional to local density of optical states (LDOS) (Yu et al., 2017). Analytical models predict that the LDOS enhancement for a system contained Ag is much more than Au. The LDOS is a fundamental quantity for the study of optical properties of disordered dielectrics and metal-dielectric composites. LDOS can alter the excitation and the emission rate of the optical emitter (Krachmalnicoff et al., 2010).

Several reviews, books and articles explain the insight of light-matter interaction in plasmonic and photonic-plasmonic systems, just to mention a few of large number of documents (Delga et al., 2014; Dong et al., 2015; Geddes, 2017; Khurgin and Sun, 2009; Marocico et al., 2016; Ranjan et al., 2017). Here, in case of PLSC device, we are interested on the through-space interactions between the metal surface (which may put on the back side of LSC device)/MNPs and luminophores. This is not the case as it is for chemical and/or electron-transfer interactions (separation will be very small of the order of few nm). In PLSC device, SPCE is possible which occurs over distances of 100 nm or more (Lakowicz, 2005). But the occurrence of SPCE is only possible when the incident light will come from metal surface side not from LSC side. In fact, the free space emission from luminophores will not be able to excite the plasmon rather will be reflected from metal surface. Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is not likely to happen for such long separation between metal and luminophores. On the contrary, localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) happens for MNPs with small size leading to a confined oscillation of the electrons as shown in the Fig. 5 . If the size of the MNPs are well below the wavelength of the incident light, the localized EM field around the MNPs occurs. The movement of free electrons is restricted by the size of colloidal metal nanoparticles or MNPs and produces local EM field enhancement. The optical properties of MNPs reasonably obey Miés theory which consider the particles to be perfect sphere and the size comparable to incident wavelength. Larger MNPs of radius “r” are preferred for metal-enhanced fluorescence, as scattering and absorption cross-section are proportional to r6 and r3, respectively. The decay processes for an optical dipole (luminophores) near a mirror (metal/MNPs layer) can easily be understood by considering the reflection of EM field back to the dipole i.e. reflective model (Kuhn, 1970).

In case of PLSC device composed of luminophores and MNPs, LSPR occurs at multiple wavelength due to the various size of MNPs, polarization of the incident light, the refractive index of surrounding medium. Theoretical calculation shows that the enhancement of emission efficiencies of point like emitter in the vicinity of metal MNPs depends on both the size of MNPs and the embedding medium. Low sized MNPs are ineffective for metal enhanced luminescence no matter what type of metal used. For example, the optimum diameter needed for Au and Ag are ~ 110 nm and ~ 55 nm, respectively, if the refractive index of medium is 1.3 (Mertens et al., 2007). Thus, researcher must keep in mind the size of the MNPs for PLSC design to get reasonable enhancement in PCE.

The device architecture of PLSC is an important aspect to make it commercially feasible. In the recent articles, several device structures for PLSC were demonstrated. We may divide them into four main groups as shown in the Fig. 6 . Here, we consider on the solid-state form of PLSC device. Though there are several reports on the PLSC with liquid form and demonstrated in the general quartz cuvette of thickness 10 mm (i.e. the path length of cuvette) (Chandra et al., 2017, 2012).

Chandra et al. have studied the plasmonic interaction on QDs for fabrication of LSC. They have shown that 53% emission enhancement can be achieved by incorporating 2 ppm Au NPs and 0.008% (weight percentage) CdSe/ZnS QDs mixture in epoxy matrix (Chandra et al., 2012). On the other hand, hybrid model which is a combination of finite difference time-domain (FDTD) and Monte-Carlo ray tracing, shows that PLSC device (where Ag MNPs as emission enhancer and scatterer) would have same efficiency with and without luminophore layers (Tummeltshammer et al., 2013). Therefore, the performance of LSC is limited by the 4n2 (n is the refractive index of wave guiding material) Yablonovitch limit scattering limit (Yablonovitch, 1982). Till now, PLSC devices suffer from low PCE and the localized nature of surface plasmon enhanced emission may be useful in near future.

Researchers must concentrate on the accomplishment of surface plasmon enhanced emission (MEF or SEF) over a large volume to improve the efficiency of PLSC devices. The easiest solution is to make a metal layer on the back surface of LSC, producing evanescent wave that will excite luminophores in its path. Ag and Au NPs are successfully used to increase the value of concentration factor for stacked PLSC (Mateen et al., 2017) as shown in the Fig. 7 (a). The PL intensity increases about 34% for diheptyl-bipyridyl-diol in the presence of Ag NPs (Saraidarov et al., 2010). The complex of europium (III) and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (Eu-EDTA) produces the strong intensification of fluorescence in presence of Ag NPs (Reisfeld et al., 2011).

Quite a few PLSC demonstrations have been reported in the last few years where researchers are utilizing plasmonic MNPs to enhance PCE as shown in the Table 1 . However, the dimension of PLSC devices are not adequate for any commercial uses. Therefore, further research activities are required to improve the size of PLSC device without jeopardizing the PCE. The best possible solution is to make up an array of small PLSC block to get reasonable PCE for the use of commercial purpose.

In most cases, Au and Ag MNPs are used for LSPR coupled emission as evident by numerous articles (Cao et al., 2012) owing to their strong LSPR responses. Like SPP, the LSPR is also sensitive to the surrounding dielectric environment (i.e. refractive index of the medium). Apart from the medium, the LSPR peak wavelength will depend on the size, shape, material of the metal MNPs. For example, 20 nm Ag MNPs show LSPR peak at ~ 400 nm (a bright yellow solution) whereas for 20 nm Au MNPs show at ~ 520 nm (a wine red color solution) in water medium (Haes and Van Duyne, 2004). Synthetic and lithographic fabrication technique can alter the LSPR peak from UV to NIR region by changing the pattern/size, material, shape, and medium. Thus, researcher can utilize the suitable MNPs or bimetallic NPs (Min and Wang, 2020) in the PLSC device for emission/absorption enhancement by matching the LSPR peak to absorption/emission peak of luminophores. Additionally, some MNPs does not show any acceptable emission/absorption enhancement inside the waveguiding matrix though it will show strong enhancement in liquid/other medium. The practical burden of using plasmonic nanostructure inside PLSC for enhancing fluorescence is the parasitic absorption and henceforth the production of local heat due to non-radiative decay channels. The ligand or dielectric shell around the plasmonic nanostructure can reduce the evanescent spatial field and limits the potential for absorption enhancement for luminophores inside LSC. Most of the PLSC devices are demonstrated with Au and Ag MNPs in recent years and enhancement factor increases up to ~ 4 fold. Cu NPs demands its usage in the PLSC device as Cu is cheaper and shows a localized plasmon band in the visible region but suffers from oxidation during the synthesis and storage (Reisfeld et al., 2014). Some bimetallic structure of Cu like Au-Cu, Pd–Cu and Ag-Cu NPs exhibit strong LSPR in visible and NIR with a few applications owing to the chemical instability of Cu. Similarly, Pd NPs shows weak LSPR in UV and Visible region, but Pd film shows strong LSPR in NIR region.

The strong coupling is the phenomena when emission energy alteration occurs in presence of MNPs. Strong coupling between SPPs and luminophores with a broad absorption spectrum is still not clearly understood and requires further study. SPP interaction is only possible for thin film PLSC device where metal film is made on the front side of waveguide in the same way as the plasmonic solar cell. The incident solar light should come from the side of thin metal film. SPP can increase the optical path length of incident light to absorb by the luminophores as it travels along the interface between the metal and waveguiding layer in PLSC device. In case of SPR, significant losses will be there due to Joule heating of the metal. In addition to this, it is difficult to achieve strong coupling even with high concentration of luminophores. The minimum distance between the luminophores and MNPs < 5 nm or less is not likely to happen for practical PLSC device to get full benefit of strong coupling. It should be noted that there are only few references of strong coupling between QDs and SPPs (Gómez et al., 2013, 2010), but number of reports are there in weak coupling regime (Achermann, 2010). Strong coupling inside the PLSC devices is not completely realized yet. For core/shell structured nanoparticles with metal as core materials shows the amplification of the luminescence due to SEF and the scattering (Segura et al., 2020) as depicted in the Fig. 7(b). Weak coupling regime induces PL modification by changing the shape/polarization, intensity enhancement/quenching, alteration of radiative/non-radiative decay rate, etc. Therefore, researchers may enhance the emission or PLQY by using metal/MNPs based on the theory of MEF, SEF, and SHINEF (based on weak coupling regime).

To improve the PCE of PLSC device, researcher should concentrate on the five essential factors - (i) emission enhancement of luminophores in the vicinity of MNPs/metal surface, (ii) retainment of optical properties of metal and luminophores inside the waveguide matrix, (iii) the improvement of waveguiding properties of matrix in presence of foreign elements, (iv) optimized device architectures, and (v) cost optimization for commercial feasibility. Several researches are already done on the topic of emission enhancement of luminophores due to MNPs, metal surface and bimetallic structures. Now, researchers can easily demonstrate PLSC device with several alternative metal-luminophore system and may improve the PCE. Device architecture would be another important strategy to improve the PCE of PLSC device. Careful use of MNPs or bimetallic NPs or bulk metal in PLSC device in the form of thin film, stacked-layered, tandem configuration or composite form together with the luminophores would be advantageous to obtain a decent PCE to make the device practical in near future.

In this review, we mostly discuss the basic mechanism of general LSC device and try to portray the possible ways to improve the PCE. In this scenario, the plasmonic may play an important role to enhance the PCE for PLSC device though several restrictions are there to implement in practical device. Years of research in PLSC device with different luminophores and structure, a moderate power conversion efficiencies (PCE) in the range of 2%–7% have accomplished with a common threshold PCE of 10% (Aghaei et al., 2020; Reinders et al., 2018; van Sark et al., 2014). More research must be done collectively on the luminophores (mainly QDs), the waveguiding materials and device architectures. In most instances, PLSC devices typically use < 0.01 wt% (~10-6 mol/cm3) of Au/Ag along with luminophores for plasmonic enhancement. Therefore, it is not too expensive for thin film PLSC as very less amount of plasmonic materials is required to obtain the effect. Additionally, researchers can use alternative materials other than noble metals in PLSC, then cost will be a little less. Other plasmonic materials like Cu, bimetallic NPS of Cu, heavily doped semiconductors (oxide, nitride), 2D materials (graphene) can also be used for PLSC device. SP in nonmetallic structures behaves differently but shows relatively low loss compared to metals. Last but not the least, cost optimization must be required for the economic viability of PLSCs device (Wu et al., 2018).

The aim of the current research on waveguiding materials has driven towards the development of the LSC device which will be large, durable, friendly to the outdoor environment and especially fulfil the demand for higher PCE. It is expected that researchers will validate the large‐area LSC/PLSC devices and materialize the ideal of BIPV soon. In recent years, numerous new types of waveguiding materials are developed in addition to the glass and commodity polymers. They have some interesting properties including chemical inertness, optical clarity, chemical tunability, light-weight, (super)hydrophobic, oleophobic, thermally/electrically responsive (Griffini, 2019; Griffini et al., 2013; Zettl et al., 2017). In addition to this, more and more waveguiding materials will be available for PLSC device which will host both luminophores and MNPs without any loss of functionality and inherent properties. Or else it will continue to exist as a laboratory technology with no significance. No one will show any interest on the device in future. Thus, progressively more research is expected for the improvement of PCE and dimension of PLSC device in near future. We hope this article would encourage the research of PLSC devices as there exist all the raw materials to turn up the idea.
